topic: Introduction to interactivity using environmental variables like mouse position, previous mouse position, and boolean states for mouse and keyboard input.

concepts.name: mouseX and mouseY
concepts.explanation: System-defined variables that track the current X and Y coordinates of the cursor on the canvas.
concepts.why_it_matters: They allow you to link visual elements directly to the user's movement.

concepts.name: pmouseX and pmouseY
concepts.explanation: Variables that store the mouse coordinates from the previous frame. The instructor explains it as a 'copy' of the information that happens at the end of a frame, meaning they are always one frame behind.
concepts.why_it_matters: Essential for drawing continuous lines or paths because you can connect the current position to where the mouse just was.

concepts.name: mouseIsPressed
concepts.explanation: A boolean variable that is 'true' when the mouse button is held down and 'false' otherwise.
concepts.why_it_matters: Allows for conditional drawing, such as only drawing when the user is actively clicking.

concepts.name: keyPressed
concepts.explanation: A boolean variable that is 'true' when any key on the keyboard is being held down.
concepts.why_it_matters: Enables multi-modal interactivity where the user can change visual properties (like color) using the keyboard while drawing with the mouse.

concepts.name: Visual Persistence
concepts.explanation: By moving the background() function from the draw loop to the setup function, the canvas is no longer cleared every frame, allowing previous drawings to stay on screen.
concepts.why_it_matters: Crucial for creating drawing programs or 'trails' rather than just moving single objects.

code_examples.demonstrates: Drawing a continuous line that follows the cursor by connecting the previous position to the current one.
code_examples.common_issue: If you don't use pmouseX/Y, the line will only draw from the origin (0,0) to the mouse, resulting in a 'starburst' effect rather than a continuous path.

code_examples.demonstrates: Using mathematical logic to map mouse position to a visual property (circle size).
code_examples.common_issue: Forgetting parentheses around the subtraction will cause the division to happen before the subtraction due to order of operations.

code_examples.demonstrates: Using an if/else statement to switch between drawing dots (when clicking) and lines (when not clicking).
code_examples.common_issue: Placing the stroke/fill commands in the wrong order relative to the drawing command.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: My drawing disappears or doesn't leave a trail.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: The background() function is inside the draw() loop, clearing the canvas every frame.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Move background() to the setup() function so it only clears once at the start.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The math for my circle size is wrong.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Order of operations (PEMDAS/BODMAS) - the division is happening before the subtraction.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Wrap the subtraction in parentheses: `(mouseX - mouseY) / 10`.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The line is being drawn but the circle isn't appearing or looks weird.
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: The order of drawing commands is incorrect (e.g., calling noStroke() after the shape).
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Ensure style commands (stroke, fill) are called before the shape they are meant to affect.

key_moments.description: Lowering the frame rate to 10 FPS to make the movement of pmouseX/Y visible and easier to understand.
The instructor demonstrates how moving background() to setup creates persistence, turning a 'moving object' into a 'drawing tool'.
Using console.log to debug the boolean states of mouseIsPressed and keyPressed simultaneously.

prerequisites: Basic understanding of setup() and draw() loops
Fundamental knowledge of if/else conditional statements
Basic understanding of coordinate systems (X and Y)
student_questions: How do I make a line that follows my mouse?
Why is my drawing disappearing every time the mouse moves?
How can I make a shape change color when I press a key?
What is the difference between mouseX and pmouseX?
How do I make a drawing program where you only draw when clicking?
Why is my math for the circle size not working correctly?
How do I check if a key is being held down?
Why are my shapes all overlapping or leaving trails?
How can I see if my code actually knows the mouse is pressed?
How do I make a circle that gets bigger or smaller based on where my mouse is?
